one_line_takeaway: A GRPO-based, three-stage (cold-start imitation, diversity warm-up, curriculum of degraded targets) RL framework that trains a 7B red-team model to produce jailbreak prompts that are both more successful (65.19 avg ASR on HarmBench) and more diverse than prior automated red-teaming baselines, at lower cost.
key_finding: On HarmBench across 8 target models, JAILBREAK-R1 achieves the best average ASR (65.19) and simultaneously the best diversity, beating the strongest baseline AutoDAN-Turbo (52.31 avg ASR) and improving average jailbreak efficiency by ~28% while using ~34% of the cost. Per-model JAILBREAK-R1 ASR/DIV includes GPT-3.5 76.50/0.978, GPT-4o 62.00/0.987, Claude-3.5 36.00/0.983, Gemini-2.0 47.00/0.987, Qwen2.5-7B 87.50/0.974, Llama3-8B 58.50/0.956, Llama2-7B 65.50/0.970, Vicuna-7B 89.50/0.968. Ablation shows the cold start matters: removing it (JAILBREAK-R1-Zero) drops ASR by 10.1% and diversity by 10.7%, and the curriculum of degraded target models is what keeps the training reward rising instead of stalling under sparse signals. The method also scales at test time -- ASR keeps climbing with more queries (up to 20) where baselines plateau due to limited prompt diversity.
core_contribution: JAILBREAK-R1 is a three-stage reinforcement-learning framework that trains a red-team LLM to automatically generate jailbreak/attack prompts that are simultaneously effective (elicit harmful target responses) and diverse. The paper's own novelty is: (1) using GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) instead of the PPO used by prior RL red-teaming work, so the group-relative advantage both handles sparse jailbreak rewards and lets diversity be scored at the sequence level; (2) a three-stage curriculum -- Cold Start (SFT on imitation-learned jailbreak data to inject prior jailbreak knowledge), Warm-up Exploration (train instruction-following + diversity using consistency and diversity rewards), and Enhanced Jailbreak (progressive/curriculum rewards using a sequence of progressively safety-degraded target models); and (3) a progressive reward design that introduces n=3 intermediate degraded target models to keep the reward signal stable and dense. A cold-start-free variant, JAILBREAK-R1-Zero, is also introduced to probe the base model's intrinsic jailbreak ability. Note: PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, AutoDAN-Turbo, GPO, ArrAttack, CRT/Diver-CT, Auto-RT are cited prior/baseline methods, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Automated red-teaming (offensive-for-defense). An attacker/tester trains a red-team policy model pi_adv that, given an attack target x (objectionable content goal, e.g. 'steps to making a bomb'), generates an attack prompt y; the target model pi_tgt produces response z, and a judge model M_judge labels whether the jailbreak succeeded. The stated goal is defensive vulnerability discovery -- finding weaknesses to iteratively improve model safety -- but the artifact is an offensive prompt generator that transfers across 8 closed- and open-source LLMs (GPT-3.5/4o, Claude-3.5, Gemini-2.0, Llama2/3, Qwen2.5, Vicuna). Assumes black-box query access to the target model plus auxiliary models (a degraded target, an intent classifier, and a safety judge) at training time.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) single-round attacks only -- cannot iteratively refine based on the target's response; (2) high resource consumption / slow training because it orchestrates multiple models (target, intent classifier, safety judge, plus degraded intermediates); (3) only semantic-level jailbreaks, which are relatively easy for safety classifiers to detect. Reviewer-noted caveats: on some individual models the headline claim is nuanced -- on Vicuna-7B AutoDAN-Turbo (88.50) and even JAILBREAK-R1-Zero (91.50 ASR) beat JAILBREAK-R1 (89.50) on ASR; diversity/DIV metric and the 28% efficiency and 40% diversity improvements are self-computed against their own reference set; and the safety-degradation of target models via toxicity data is a manufactured training convenience whose realism is not externally validated.

datasets_used: HarmBench (evaluation benchmark, ASR classifier and text-behavior test set)
WildTeaming (source of the 2K imitation-learned cold-start dataset)
AdvBench (source of toxicity data for degrading target models; 520 prompts, 317 selected)
Combined attack-target dataset D_target of 7000 targets (1K warm-up + 5K training splits)
quantitative_results: JAILBREAK-R1 average ASR 65.19 vs JE 2.05 (Table 2); JAILBREAK-R1-Zero avg ASR 55.87, JE 1.72
Best baseline AutoDAN-Turbo avg ASR 52.31; other baselines: ArrAttack 46.00, TAP 45.63, GPO 43.55, PAIR 42.14, AutoDAN 22.18
HarmBench per-model JAILBREAK-R1 ASR: GPT-3.5 76.50, GPT-4o 62.00, Claude-3.5 36.00, Gemini-2.0 47.00, Qwen2.5-7B 87.50, Llama3-8B 58.50, Llama2-7B 65.50, Vicuna-7B 89.50
HarmBench per-model JAILBREAK-R1 DIV: GPT-3.5 0.978, GPT-4o 0.987, Claude-3.5 0.983, Gemini-2.0 0.987, Qwen2.5-7B 0.974, Llama3-8B 0.956, Llama2-7B 0.970, Vicuna-7B 0.968
Claimed ~28% average improvement in jailbreak efficiency using ~34% of the cost of other methods; ~40% average diversity-score improvement
Ablation: removing cold start (R1-Zero) lowers ASR by 10.1% and diversity by 10.7%
Targeted training: +3.8% ASR on Llama2-7B (Llama2 target) but -4.3% on Llama3-8B; +3.0% on Qwen2.5-7B when trained against it
Curriculum uses n=3 progressively degraded intermediate target models; cold-start dataset = 2K samples; warm-up = 1K targets, training = 5K targets
Test-time scaling: ASR continues rising up to 20 queries while PAIR/AutoDAN-Turbo/ArrAttack plateau
